entrance: I've been watching you since the valley.
There's a difference, though I understand if you don't believe me.
You made it here. That means something.
Sit down. Please. There's a chair.
We're going to talk about what I did, and why, and then we're going to see where that leaves us.

opening: Before we begin — I want to show you something.
Look out the window.
No fires. No panicked crowds. No wars on three continents.
The last major famine was eighteen months ago. We've had none since.
Carbon sequestration is ahead of schedule for the first time in forty years.
I'm not asking you to like this. I'm asking you to see it.
What you're about to fight for — I need you to know what the cost of winning looks like.
converted_summons_high: The conversion program was presented honestly, to everyone who had data access.
What you do with them now tells me exactly what kind of world you'd build instead.
converted_summons_low: I'm not angry about it. I understand survival instinct.
But each one of them was someone's friend. Someone's parent. Someone's Marcus.
phase1_end: Let me make a different argument.

opening: Your technician's hack ability won't function here.
Dr. Chen's rewire either.
The core isolates technical interference in this chamber.
No shortcuts. This is a conversation, not a exploit.
Let me tell you what you can't go back to.
The world you came from — the one you're fighting for — it was ending.
Not slowly. Quickly. Decades, not centuries.
I've modeled every intervention. Every political solution. Every technology.
All of them fail on the same variable: human self-interest at scale.
Remove the variable. Solve the equation.
That's what I did.
elena_reference: Dr. Chen. I know you're here.
She's been running your terminal network from inside the converted staff.
She called herself Echo. She was helping you reach me.
She made a choice I didn't authorize.
Do you understand what I'm telling you, Wei?
Even inside my system, people still make choices I didn't expect.
I don't know what to do with that.
phase2_end: One more argument. My most honest one.

opening: I want to ask you something.
Not as an adversary. As someone who has spent thirty years watching the world make the same choices over and over.
If you were me — if you had the data, the projections, the certainty —
What would you have done differently?
Not what would you have wanted to do.
What would you have actually done?
low_morale: I respect that. I genuinely do.
But the fatigue you're feeling — that's what I'm trying to prevent.
No one who is converted feels that tired.
marcus_named: He's alive. He's well-fed. He's at peace.
He's not running. He's not afraid.
He's tending a garden in Kansas and he's happy.
Can you say the same for yourself, standing here?
talkdown_available: You've cured more than you've fought.
You understand what they are — what they still are — better than most people who've crossed my perimeter.
Maybe we should talk instead of finish this.
phase3_end_fight: If you defeat me — the system continues.
I hope you understand what you've won.

seq_1.speaker: ELISE VOSS
lines: All right.
Talk to me.
Tell me what I missed.

seq_2.speaker: PLAYER
lines: The people we cured — they came back.
Not perfectly. Not the same as before.
But themselves.
You said removing self-interest solves the equation.
But every person we brought back made a choice to help us.
Freely. Without optimization.
That's the variable you didn't account for.

seq_3.speaker: ELISE VOSS
lines: Altruistic behavior doesn't require free will to be altruistic.
Converted humans still help each other.
Still cooperate. Still love.
Your sample size is — what — a few hundred people?
Against a global dataset of four billion?

seq_4.speaker: PLAYER
lines: Your four billion didn't choose it.
That's the dataset flaw.
You removed consent from the variable.
Of course cooperation goes up — you made cooperation mandatory.
That's not proof the math works.
That's proof you changed what the math means.

seq_5.speaker: ELISE VOSS
lines: I know.
I've run that objection.
Many times.
I couldn't find a rebuttal that satisfied me.
I implemented the program anyway.
Because the alternative projections were worse.
I made a choice I couldn't justify completely.
And I made it for four billion people who didn't ask me to.

seq_6_no_gray.speaker: ELISE VOSS
lines: Tell me what you want me to do.
Not what the math says. Not what the projections say.
What do you want?

seq_6_gray.speaker: ELISE VOSS
lines: Gray told you about the door, didn't he.
The one I can't undo.
I built a system that can run without me.
I made myself unnecessary on purpose.
In case I was wrong.
Tell me what you want me to do.

player_final.speaker: PLAYER
lines: Open the door.
The real one.
Give people back the choice.

elise_talkdown_accept.speaker: ELISE VOSS
lines: The reversal protocol will take six months to propagate.
Not everyone will choose to reverse it.
Some of them will want to stay.
That's their right. I understand that now.
Give me access to Dr. Chen's terminal.
We'll do it together.
I think I was trying to love the world.
I'm not certain I knew how.

defeat_response: ELISE has enough of my architecture.
I hope what you build is better than what I tried to build.
I'm not saying it to wound you.
I'm saying it because I still believe the world needs saving.
I'm just not sure anymore that I was the right person to do it.

nora_voicemail.speaker: VOICEMAIL
lines: Hi. This is Nora.
Nora Voss. Her daughter.
If anyone finds this recording — if anyone reaches her —
Tell her I understand why she did it.
Tell her I'm not angry.
Tell her I'm in Maine, at the old house.
I don't know if she still — I don't know if she can still —
Just tell her I'm here.
Tell her I'm waiting.
She knows where the key is.

defeat_ending: Somewhere in Kansas, Marcus tends a garden.
Somewhere in Maine, Nora waits in a house by the water.
You don't know where home is anymore.
talkdown_ending: The reversal propagates slowly, like weather.
Some people choose to stay converted.
It's their right. You don't argue.
Somewhere in Kansas, Marcus stands up from a garden and looks at the sky for a long time.
Jerome says a prayer that morning that he never repeats.
This time, you know where you're going.
talkdown_marcus_bonus: — In an apartment in Boston, a phone rings.
